tweets.text:
RT @bentlegen: Coding isn't holding you back. Product work is.

Introducing Modem, your dev team's auto-triage PM.

It listens to user sign…
nothing pairs better with a productive morning than cutting-edge aerospace and defense systems from raytheon

when you think of feature A your job is to recognize that it's actually a special case of feature X and if you implement feature X it unlocks feature A B C

people are struggling with this lately because they can't think bigger than what their agent can do

so many companies both established and startup are trying to get a foothold on something ai related

so now whenever there's any new ai concept no matter how small there's a massive pile on

doesn't help that you can shit out code now for any idea
if i have a mac that can be always on what is the best solution to use the messages app from web on a non-mac device?

in the early days of SaaS you were going after customers who weren't already using something so it was kind of easy to get users

people started giving advice on how to build products and would say "i'm successful listen to me"

but most of the advice was completely wrong because things look totally different 10 years later in a world where software has proliferated

a similar thing is happening now with AI tools - everyone is hungry to try new workflows so it's relatively easy to get users

the same dynamic is playing out again, there's terrible advice proliferating with "but we have users" as validation

it should actually be a negative signal because they can't actually explain their thoughts

we've never done code review

but damn if your team is producing this much code you're using LLMs entirely incorrectly

no one struggles with large amounts of code more than an LLM, if you don't keep that in check you have a self defeating codebase

this thing about open source projects making their tests private

you're either open source or not - part of that is enabling a successor to show up for net benefit of the world

making that intentionally difficult is fine but then it's something different

could have prompted a git worktree feature into existence a while ago

but we put in the time to design a system that can also support docker containers, remote sandboxes, etc

with sync that can tolerate going offline

we're always the last to ship, but we're the last to ship
